text: Storm surge disasters are one of the most severe disasters in China's coastal areas. In recent years, the economic losses caused by them have accounted for more than 90% of the total losses from Marine disasters. Therefore, it is particularly important to construct a simple and accurate loss estimation model. Based on the existing research on storm surge disasters, this paper establishes a BP neural network model optimized based on beetle antennae search and applies it to the pre-assessment of economic losses caused by storm surge disasters. This paper collected 29 relatively well-recorded storm surge disaster loss data in Fujian Province from 1994 to 2016, established a pre-assessment index system for storm surge disaster losses, and preprocessed the index factors using the entropy method to eliminate the influence of redundant data information on prediction. Simulation tests were conducted on the model. The results show that, compared with the standard BP neural network, the new model effectively avoids the possibility of the network falling into local minima. Moreover, compared with conventional optimization algorithms, it overcomes the disadvantages of long training time and slow convergence speed, and has better robustness and prediction accuracy.

A numerical prediction model suitable for storm surges in the Yangtze River estuary area was established by using the Estuarine Coastal Ocean Model (ECOM Si). This mode adopts a natural orthogonal horizontal coordinate system that has a good fitting ability for the shoreline and a vertical σ coordinate system that can distinguish more complex seabed topography. The model takes into account the influence of the caliber flow of the Yangtze River on storm surges, and partially considers the impact of the nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges on storm water increase. The atmospheric forced field for storm surge forecasting uses the model pressure field and model wind field. Eight case simulations of typhoon storm surges in the Yangtze River estuary area were conducted using the established model. The average absolute error was less than 10cm when comparing the simulated water increase with the measured peak water increase. Using the model established in this study, numerical experiments were conducted on the sensitivity of meteorological factors to storm surge levels. The test results show that a reduction (increase) of 20hPa in the central pressure of a typhoon can lead to an increase (or decrease) of approximately 100cm in the storm surge level. The influence of the maximum wind speed radius error of a typhoon on the variation of typhoon water increase is also relatively significant. Experiments also show that doubling (halving) the runoff of the Yangtze River can cause an average increase of 25cm(decrease of 13cm) in storm surges. Numerical experiments on the influence of astronomical tide phase changes on storm water increase show that when storm surges and astronomical tides interact at different phases, the maximum increase in storm surge level can reach 70cm or decrease by 90cm.

Shallow seas are particularly conducive to the development of storm surges. Therefore, the study of storm surges in shallow seas is of great significance to coastal countries and regions that are prone to storm surge disasters. Based on the fundamental dynamic characteristics of shallow sea storm surges, this paper derives the dimensionless equations of three-dimensional shallow sea storm surges and presents the characteristic parameters for measuring the inertial oscillation effect and nonlinear effect. It is pointed out that the theory of shallow sea storm surges can be divided into the ordinary shallow sea theory and the 'ultra-shallow sea' theory, and the dynamic conditions for this division are given. As an example, the article also points out that the research on storm surges in the Bohai Sea should fall within the scope of the 'ultra-shallow sea' theory. The zero-order model and first-order model of the three-dimensional problem of storm surges in the Bohai Sea are presented in the text, and the corresponding forecast equations for storm surge levels and storm currents are derived. In addition, for the continental shelf situation, the corresponding analytical expressions of storm surge levels and storm currents were obtained and analyzed. This paper also studies the mutual adaptation problem between the flow field and the pressure field in the 'ultra-shallow sea' under the action of wind stress.

During the storm surge in Taiwan, the intertidal zone is generally washed out first, then it silted down on the surface of the local beach. Typhoon accumulation beneath the sand layer lies an uneven erosion surface. At the place where siltation occurs more quickly, cave-dwelling animals are buried and die. Therefore, in marine strata, the extensive scouring surface and the changes in lithology and organisms that occurred in it are not necessarily caused by changes in the sea surface; it might also be caused by short-term abnormal events such as storm waves. The storm surge sedimentary layer has interlaced lamination and wave-scarred lamination. A gust of wind storm surge sand layer thickness is about centimeters. During a typhoon surge, the waterway connectivity on tidal flats or sandbars has undergone partial changes. Tidal waterways commonly expand throughout with strengthened side corrosion effect. The trend of planar swing depends on the nature of the waterway and the stability of the waterway before the typhoon. Near tidal waterways that have tended to decline or silt up, they can become active again during the storm surge. Tidal ditch changes have diversity; generally speaking, the siltation at the head of the ditch is severe, even clogging, while the lower part is significantly widened and the bottom elevation of the trough has been raised. The impact of winter storm water increase on high beaches and tidal waterways is very similar to a typhoon storm surge. It enhances the activity of tidal channels, strengthens the side corrosion effect, and can even create erosion pits that spread out along the current on the salt cylinder beach. Large high shoals far from the waterway silt up during winter storms, with siltation height in one tide reaching one millimeter. In contrast, beach surfaces relatively close to large waterways were eroded, and multiple drops on the high beach intensified the cutting erosion of high beach gullies. If a winter storm zone experiences low temperatures, then frost-swelling mounds may appear in the subtidal zone close to the high tide. The entire intertidal zone and the subtidal area near the low-tide level become covered with ice. Under the action of the onshore wind, an ice superimposed belt can be formed by the movement of ice blocks towards the shore, creating planing marks and causing the movement of mud and sand towards the shore and along the shore.

With global warming, storm surge disasters in coastal areas are becoming increasingly severe. The construction of resilient cities with disaster adaptability has become a research hotspot, and landscape infrastructure is gradually playing an increasingly important role in cities, with great potential in the construction of storm surge adaptability. How to introduce the concept of resilient cities to enhance the adaptability of cities to storm surges through landscape infrastructure has become the starting point of this study. The article first analyzes concepts such as resilient cities, landscape infrastructure and adaptability, then traces the formation and development process of the resilient city theory and summarizes its connotation and characteristics, and puts forward inspirations for modern urban planning and construction. Then, an analysis of storm surges is conducted, including their formation causes, hazards and influencing factors. The history of human resistance and adaptation to storm surges is also explored. The current main response measures are summarized and the existing problems are analyzed. Then, the article analyzes the expansion of the content and the transformation of the role of landscape infrastructure under ecologicalism and landscape urbanism. Combined with the characteristics of landscape infrastructure and the principle of resilient cities, it points out that landscape infrastructure will play an important role in the construction of resilient cities in response to disasters. Subsequently, the framework system of resilient landscape infrastructure is proposed. Subsequently, the article explores the landscape infrastructure strategies for adapting to storm surges from four perspectives: The first perspective is the theoretical framework, which elaborates respectively under the framework of 'module, network, and dimension', reflecting the theoretical guidance of the combination of resilient cities and landscape infrastructure; The second perspective is the function of landscape infrastructure in storm surges, which can be classified into shock adaptability, flood adaptability and saline-alkali adaptability based on the analysis of storm surges. The third perspective is the spatial distribution perspective, which explores waterfront parks, beaches, inland parks, nature reserves and streets that are more directly related to storm surges, and respectively elaborates on their different functions. The fourth perspective is the driving factor perspective, which expands the research scope to abstract systems related to the construction of storm surge adaptive landscape infrastructure, including disaster risk assessment and detection, the formulation of policies and regulations, and public participation. Then, the article takes New York as a case study, summarizes its experiences and lessons through the research on typical landscape infrastructure, thereby testing the theoretical part of the previous text and making supplements. Based on the theory of resilient cities, this article guides the research on strategies for landscape infrastructure to cope with storm surges. At the same time, it expands and extends the connotation of resilient cities in combination with the characteristics of landscape infrastructure. The two promote each other, and takes practice as reflection to guide the application of the theory in reality.

The United Nations International Decade for Disaster Reduction proposes that greater attention should be paid to the risks caused by natural disasters and the corresponding disaster prevention plans. After more than a decade of development, storm surge disaster risk assessment has gradually formed a unique research field. At present, research on storm surge disaster risk assessment both at home and abroad mainly focuses on numerical forecasting of storm surges, estimation of tide height and recurrence period studies, hazard assessment, exposure and vulnerability assessment of disaster-bearing bodies, disaster risk zoning, and assessment of disaster losses. However, there are relatively few studies on the comprehensive risk of storm surge disasters and the risk of secondary disaster chains in coastal cities. Establishing a risk assessment index system for storm surge disasters in coastal cities from multiple perspectives such as nature, society, economy, policy, culture and engineering, and conducting comprehensive disaster risk research on storm surges in coastal cities using 3S technology is an important direction in current storm surge disaster research. The future research focus should be on constructing a scenario-based storm surge disaster risk assessment method system and developing a risk-based decision support system, strengthening the research on the compilation of disaster evacuation and refuge maps, establishing a continuous urban disaster risk management system, and conducting quantitative pre-assessment of disaster prevention plans and disaster losses, etc.

The Bohai Sea storm surge caused by the cold wave or cold air in northern China, according to the analysis in the literature, belongs to the type of 'ultra-shallow sea storm surge'. Taking the Bohai Sea tidal wave as an example, conducting numerical calculations based on the theory of ultra-shallow sea storm surges not only serves as a powerful test for this theory but also provides a useful reference for exploring the mechanism and forecast of the Bohai Sea tidal wave. Reproducing the strong wind field caused by the cold front on the computational grid is obviously a rather difficult task when the on-site sea surface wind data is extremely scarce. This paper only designs several theoretical model wind stress fields based on several main mechanical characteristics of the wind field above the Bohai Sea when the Bohai Sea wind wave occurs, and conducts numerical calculations on the Bohai Sea wind wave, with the aim of obtaining an overall picture and main features of the entire process. The calculation results achieved this expected goal.

Typhoon storm surges in each recurrence period mainly cause relatively severe impacts within an area of 1-2 kilometers along the river. Storm surges that occur once in 50 years are considered high-risk, while the rest are classified as medium-risk. At extreme tide levels, the inundation radius exceeds 4 kilometers, the inundation area reaches 374.19 square kilometers, the maximum inundation depth is 6.84 meters, and the total economic loss amounts to 89.4 billion yuan. It may cause catastrophic impacts and is an unacceptable risk. As one of the regions most severely affected by typhoon and storm surge disasters in the world, China's coastal areas urgently need to establish a scientific and practical risk assessment paradigm. Although this paper has conducted a relatively systematic study on the theories, methods and cases of typhoon storm surge disaster risk assessment, due to the complexity of the involved variable factors, there are still many bottlenecks in the existing research methods. Therefore, in the future, further deepening should be carried out in the fields of multi-disaster scenario simulation and comprehensive risk assessment, catastrophe risk assessment and response, multi-temporal and spatial scale risk assessment and scale transformation Conduct research to provide a basis for disaster reduction, risk reduction and management decision-making in coastal areas.

Through research, it has been found that  the coastal area of tianjin is one of the regions with the most frequent and severe storm surges in the world. Storm surge disasters occur throughout the year . Besides typhoon storm surge disasters in summer, there are also disastrous temperate storm surges in spring, autumn and winter. Observation facts show that there is a possibility of disastrous storm surges occurring in every month of the year along the coast of Tianjin. The research in this paper provides reliable historical data for the forecasting and moisture-proofing of the temperate storm surge along the coast of Tianjin. To meet the requirements of numerical model calculation for temperate storm surges, the authors have made successful improvements in the calculation of the temporal and spatial distribution of the model pressure field and wind field  and obtained satisfactory results. The results of numerical simulation show that the model proposed in this paper can be applied to the engineering calculation of temperate storm surges , and by using the high-quality pressure field forecast products released by developed countries available, the forecast pressure field and wind field required for the numerical model calculation of temperate storm surges are calculated based on the method proposed in this paper . This model also has the ability to predict.

The computational area of the high-resolution nested grid storm surge numerical prediction model developed in this paper not only includes almost the entire East China Sea, it also includes inland waters such as the Yangtze River estuary and the adjacent inland terrain to ensure sufficient resolution for the storm surge sensitive areas along the shore. On every pattern grid point, whether it is the water depth of the continental shelf sea area, bays, rivers flowing into the sea, or the topography of the land, all originate from sea maps, topographic maps, or municipal engineering drawings. In order to make the computing domain reflect the true form of nature as accurately as possible and calculate more accurately the full picture of the typhoon's forced force field and storm surge, the six typhoon processes reported in the post-test cover typical typhoon types that have an impact on the coastal areas of Shanghai. The test results indicate that the numerical model and post-model reporting accuracy established can basically meet the needs of typhoon storm surge forecasting along the coast of Shanghai. It needs to be emphasized here that the improvement of the accuracy of storm surge forecasts is in step with the enhancement of meteorological forecast standards. The 24-hour forecast error of typhoon positions by domestic and foreign meteorological departments was around 180 kilometers, and the forecast error of typhoon intensity was about 10. The forecast error for the 24-hour typhoon landfall time is estimated by Dr. Jelesnianski, an American expert, to be 6 hours. This makes the accurate forecast period for typhoon storm surges cannot be too long. It should also be pointed out that currently, meteorological forecasting departments at home and abroad have the same understanding of typhoons, but the position and intensity given at a certain moment are also not the same, or even vary greatly. Therefore, to evaluate the merits of a storm surge forecasting model, it should be observed whether the model can provide satisfactory results when the typhoon path and parameters are accurate or relatively accurate. The forecasting department should conduct simulation calculations and forecasts to master the performance of the model. Over the past decade, foreign countries have achieved many results in the application of storm surge models. Storm surge models are not only for forecasting reference, but are also applied in areas such as storm surge disaster assessment and floodplain risk analysis [1]. For the production of coastal storm surge disaster maps and vulnerability risk maps, they provide a scientific basis for coastal zone functional zoning and flood control emergency management. Therefore, it is suggested that the coastal economic region of our country should promptly carry out research and application in this main aspect to better serve the national economy and disaster prevention and mitigation.

Analysis and research reveal that the coastal area of Tianjin is one of the regions with the most frequent and severe storm surges in the world. Storm surges occur in all four seasons of the year. In summer, typhoons and storm surges mainly occur in certain months, while in spring, autumn, and winter, disastrous temperate storm surges have also been recorded. Special attention should be paid to the temperate storm surge processes occurring in specific months. Ground subsidence caused by excessive exploitation of groundwater over the years has left some coastal areas of Tianjin at sea level, or even lower than the average sea level. This has led to a reduction in the effectiveness of protective infrastructure, weakening its ability to resist storm surges and increasing the frequency of disasters. Storm surge disasters have thus become a major responsibility of the local government and a serious concern for relevant authorities and the public. Confronted with the reality of frequent and severe storm surge disasters, the coastal areas of Tianjin must enhance and improve both engineering and non-engineering measures simultaneously. In recent years, the Tianjin Municipal Government and relevant units have attached great importance to the construction of protective projects, increasing investment, and building coastal defense roads and sea barriers to enhance flood prevention capacity. Meanwhile, it is also clearly recognized that hidden dangers have not yet been completely eliminated. For example, due to ground subsidence, the height of Haihe Sluice is relatively low. During the period when Tropical Storm No. 1 affected the coastal areas of Tianjin, the peak of the rare storm surge just overtopped the sluice gate and overflowed into the urban area. Similarly, almost all quay surfaces in Tianjin New Port are low, which means the situation regarding flood prevention remains far from optimistic.

Storm surges are abnormal sea surface elevation phenomena caused by intense atmospheric disturbances of tropical and temperate wind storms, therefore, they are also called wind-induced sudden increases in water. Once the storm surge coincides with astronomical high tides, the superimposition of the two will cause the tide level to rise sharply. Combined with accompanying strong winds and huge waves, seawater often rapidly floods areas along the shore, advancing several kilometers to tens of kilometers inland in a short period of time, thus causing severe tidal disasters. The southeastern coastal area of our country is one of the regions in the world where storm surges occur most frequently. On average, multiple storm surge events occur each year, causing economic losses of at least several hundred million yuan, with some cases exceeding tens of billions of yuan. Therefore, storm surges have become one of the most severe marine disasters in our country and a key focus of national disaster prevention and mitigation work. The purpose of this study is to analyze and research historical data on storm surge disasters, conduct an initial discussion on disaster loss assessment methods, and establish a preliminary model for assessing the damage caused by storm surges in our country.

Shanghai is a hub and economic leader for opening up both domestically and internationally, as well as for exchanges between the north and the south and the east and the west in China. However, storm surge disasters are the most severe among all the disasters in this region, causing extremely serious social and economic losses. Conducting research on the risk assessment of storm surge disasters in the coastal areas of Shanghai can provide theoretical basis and scientific evidence for Shanghai to formulate risk management paradigms, emergency control plans and sustainable development models for storm surge disasters. In the key project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China 'Scenario Analysis of Emergency Plans for Natural Disaster Risks in Coastal Cities (40730526)', the project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China 'Research on the Natural Disaster Risk Assessment System of Coastal Cities in China (40571006)', and the Shanghai Science and Technology Rising Star Program project 'Research on Natural Disaster Risk Assessment and Zoning in Shanghai Based on Scenario Analysis' With the support of 'Jiu (09QA1401800)', this paper systematically analyzed the variation characteristics and formation mechanism of storm surges in Shanghai. The static water model of storm surge disaster was constructed by using the ArcGIS platform. Based on the Mike 21 hydrodynamic model and by using the interactive method of numerical calculation and spatial geographic analysis, a dynamic scenario simulation model of typhoon storm surge was constructed. From the perspectives of danger, vulnerability and exposure, the risks of storm surge disasters at different frequencies were evaluated, and a set of method systems suitable for the risk analysis and research of storm surge disasters in Shanghai was established. This paper has achieved the following eight results: 1. A risk assessment system for urban storm surge disasters suitable for the coastal areas of Shanghai has been constructed. This system is divided into three parts: risk identification, risk analysis and risk assessment. Among them, risk identification includes the analysis of storm surge characteristics, historical tide disaster analysis and disaster-causing factor analysis. Risk assessment includes hazard analysis of disaster-causing factors, vulnerability analysis of carriers and exposure analysis. 2. A simulation method based on scenario analysis was adopted to conduct risk assessment for storm surge disasters. This method differs from the traditional risk modeling based on indicators. It uses multiple spatio-temporal scenarios (time scale, spatial scale, frequency-recurrence period), natural scenarios (typhoons, storm surges, land subsidence, sea level rise), and artificial scenarios (socio-economic, population distribution) to reproduce historical tidal disasters and simulate preset tidal disasters. Static and dynamic simulations of tidal disasters were conducted respectively based on ArcGIS and Mike21 methods, making the risk expression more realistic and intuitive. 3. The typhoon storm surge disaster in Shanghai is the result of the combined effects of multiple factors including astronomy, meteorology, topography and sea level rise. It also has the following characteristics: the annual highest tide level remains consistently high, the frequency of occurrence varies from month to month, the frequency of storm surges keeps increasing, and storm surge disasters are severe. Static simulation of storm surge disasters in the coastal area of Shanghai was conducted through the ArcGIS platform. It can be known that in the scenario of damaged embankments, almost the entire study area was affected regardless of whether a once-in-a-century or once-in-a-millennium tide disaster occurred. In the case of embankments, even taking into account the impact of ground subsidence, once-in-a-millennium and once-in-a-century storm surges will not have a significant impact on the three coastal districts of Shanghai for a long time. Under the scenario of damaged embankments, the maximum inundated area of a once-in-a-century tide disaster is 1,041.45 square kilometers, accounting for 58.30% of the total area of the three coastal zones. It is located in most of Pudong, the south of Nanhui and the east of Fengxian. The maximum submergence depth is 1.50 meters, located on the tidal flats adjacent to the southeast coastal area of Fengxian District and Nanhui District. In contrast, the maximum inundated area of the once-in-a-millennium tide disaster was 1,384.97 square kilometers, accounting for 72.99% of the total area of the three coastal zones. It almost covered the entire areas of Pudong and Nanhui, with only the northwestern part of Fengxian remaining uninundated. The maximum inundation depth is 2.70 meters, located in the coastal areas of Gulu Town and Gonglu Town.

Referring to the methods for assessing inland flood losses, a loss assessment model suitable for the storm surge risk areas along the coast of Haikou Bay was established. The risk areas of storm surges along the coast of Haikou Bay were analyzed, and based on the extreme high water level of 100 years and 100 Analyze the inundation range based on the combined water level of the storm surge and the highest astronomical tide level, as well as the possible combined high water level conditions of the maximum storm surge and the highest astronomical tide level. Count the buildings in the area inundated by the extreme high water level in 100 years and estimate the possible affected population. This article makes primary and secondary classifications of the basic socio-economic data along the coast of Haikou Bay, conducts statistics item by item, and also conducts sampling surveys as needed. A detailed introduction was made to the calculation methods of the loss rates by sub-sectors. It was concluded that the loss rates by sub-sectors such as personal family property, state collective property, crops and Marine aquaculture were respectively :30%,4%,70% and 100%. Based on the social and economic data of 2001,100 Under the condition of encountering an extreme high water level, the economic loss caused by the tide disaster was calculated to be approximately 832 million yuan. The ratios of the loss amounts of individual and family property, state and collective property, crops and Marine aquaculture, casualties, and indirect losses to the total loss were 13.0%,70.0%,0.7%,0.8%, and 15.5% respectively.

Wind stress plays a decisive role in the formation of storm surges, and the wind stress drag coefficient determines the momentum transfer rate between the atmosphere and the ocean. Observation results show that the wind stress drag coefficient varies with wind speed and is related to sea surface roughness. In the paper, several expressions of wind stress drag coefficients related to wind speed are numerically simulated. Compared with the situation where they are regarded as constants, the accuracy of the calculation results has been significantly improved. By comparing the simulation results of each expression, the simulation effect using the wind stress drag coefficient formula of Smith (1980) is the best.

Vulnerability is a hot topic in the research of natural disaster risks. The vulnerability of storm surge disasters is related to the intensity of the natural process of storm surges as well as factors such as the coastal social economy, population, and natural environment. Starting from the definition of vulnerability to storm surge disasters, this paper reviews the social and physical vulnerabilities of storm surge disasters at home and abroad. It focuses on discussing the research progress of the physical vulnerability of typical disaster-bearing bodies such as population, seawalls, and houses in storm surge disasters, analyzes the uncertainties existing in the evaluation of vulnerability to storm surge disasters, and explores the role of vulnerability to storm surge disasters in the assessment of disaster losses. The application in fields such as insurance and reinsurance, and decision support for disaster prevention and mitigation has put forward the following prospects for future research on the vulnerability of storm surge disasters :(1) Develop quantitative and refined vulnerability curves that conform to the characteristics of storm surge disasters and the distribution of disaster-bearing bodies in China's coastal areas, and expand the application of storm surge vulnerability assessment results in areas such as insurance claims and disaster loss assessment; (2) Under the background of climate change, China's coastal areas are facing the risk of storm surge disasters. There is an urgent need to establish a scientific model for assessing the vulnerability of typical disaster-bearing bodies of storm surge disasters based on on-site disaster investigation, physical model experiments and numerical simulation.

In recent years, with the development of the national economy, natural disasters have affected society. The harm and losses caused are getting greater and greater, especially for those occurring in coastal areas. This is even more so for storm surge disasters! Starting from the concept of disaster risk, based on the current actual situation of storm surge disaster prevention and mitigation in our country, the following suggestions are proposed. First, strengthen the investigation and control of areas prone to marine disasters such as storm surges in our country. Classification checks should be carried out to have a clear understanding of the distribution of similar disasters in history. Counting is the top priority! The experience accumulated over the years is of great significance for disaster prevention and mitigation. The knowledge treasure house should, on the basis of previous work, cover the wind in the coastal areas of the whole country, conduct a comprehensive study on the distribution of disaster risk zoning for storm surges and establish the necessary national basic database, and different storm surge disasters should be identified for key areas with risk level distribution. Second, it is of great significance to study the internal mechanisms of the occurrence, development and disaster-causing processes of storm surges. Targeted research should be conducted, especially on those directly related to the formation of major disasters, such as the critical conditions for the formation and development of storm surges to cause disasters, and further studies should be carried out on the economic development model and other aspects in high-risk areas affected by tidal disasters. Third, make full use of modern high-tech means and vigorously develop and research storm surge disaster early warning and forecasting systems. Domestic and international discussions on the formation and development of ocean current motion and its numerical simulation already have a certain foundation. In comparison, the development abroad is more advanced. It is suggested to select appropriate targets and introduce some advanced numerical techniques from abroad to establish specialized forecasts for key areas. The warning and forecasting system should conduct real-time early warning and forecasting based on actual observation information. Fourth, carefully analyze and summarize foreign experiences, especially learning from countries where storm surge disasters occur frequently and disaster prevention measures are relatively complete. Based on this experience and in light of China's national conditions, new methods and measures for preventing and controlling storm surge disasters should be researched. Fifth, carry out popular education on the basic knowledge of storm surge disaster prevention and control, including the basic conditions for the occurrence and development of storm surge disasters and the measures to reduce disaster losses when they occur. In coastal areas where storm surge disasters occur frequently, measures should be taken based on the risk level of storm surges. The site selection and development of industrial and mining enterprises, villages and towns, railways, highways, bridges, and reservoirs should consider the storm surge risks in their location, and corresponding preventive measures, including engineering measures, should be taken to formulate disaster reduction and preparedness plans. In some areas prone to storm surge disasters, it is also necessary to carry out necessary disaster preparedness drills based on the actual situation to truly achieve preparedness for any eventuality. Sixth, referring to the inland flood management model, a flood insurance and risk control system should be established to enable risk sharing and dispersion. Marine disasters usually occur in coastal areas where the economy is relatively developed, providing a certain foundation for carrying out disaster prevention insurance. At present, it is necessary to devote efforts to researching the risk mechanism of storm surges.

The nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges in Shanghai caused by eight tropical cyclones with different paths was numerically studied by using two-dimensional nonlinear storm surge, astronomical tide and joint water level models. The water increase characteristics caused by the nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges were discussed. The contributions of each nonlinear term in the control equation to the water level changes caused by the nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges were analyzed. Research shows that after considering the nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges, the numerical simulation results of storm surges and water levels have been improved. Nonlinear bottom friction plays an important role in controlling the nonlinear interaction between astronomical tides and storm surges, while nonlinear shallow water terms and momentum convective transport cannot be ignored in the nonlinear interaction between the two tides.

The two-dimensional hydrodynamic model Mike21 was applied to numerically simulate the influence of the river mouth control coating at the Qiantang River estuary on the storm surge in Hangzhou Bay. Firstly, the main factors influencing the impact of enclosure on storm surges were analyzed using generalized terrain. Then, the changes of storm surges before and after the existing enclosure under extreme and once-in-a-century conditions were calculated using actual terrain. Finally, the impacts caused by the planned enclosure in the future were predicted. The results show that the change of the planar boundary caused by the enclosure is the main factor affecting the storm surge in Hangzhou Bay, while the influence of the topographic uplift caused by the enclosure is the secondary factor. The existing enclosure has raised the high point of the extreme storm in Hangzhou Bay by 1.85 to 0.09 meters, and the lifting amplitude decreases from the top of the bay to the mouth. After the planning and reclamation, the high tide level of the Hangzhou Bay storm rose overall, with the greatest increase in the middle part of the bay being 0.25 meters.

Waves and storm surges are important Marine disaster factors. In the past, people mainly studied these disaster factors themselves, while there was very little research on the disaster-bearing bodies they act on. In fact, Marine disasters only occur when the disaster-bearing bodies they act on are damaged. The purpose of this study is to propose risk assessment standards, methods and procedures for the flood damage caused by wave storm surges, providing a scientific basis for coastal disaster prevention and mitigation. For the Yellow River Delta demonstration area, based on the degree of embankment overflow, the risk level standards for embankment overflow disasters were proposed. And based on the established numerical models of wave and storm surge tides and the long-term prediction results, the risk assessment methods and program steps were put forward. A risk assessment was conducted on the main sections of the embankments in the nearshore waters of the Yellow River Delta for multi-year and typical typhoon process flood disasters. The result obtained is that the sea dikes along the coast of this area generally do not experience water level overflow below the storm surge water level. Only when combined with the action of waves will seawater overflow occur. When a once-in-50-years storm surge or wave occurs, the risk of storm surge or wave damage to most seawalls is at level 3 to 4, meaning that the effective wave height has climbed up or is close to reaching the top of the seawall. The storm surges and wave disasters caused by typhoons No. 9216 and No. 9711 occur approximately once every 150 to 200 years. From the actual situation, the risk assessment standards and methods for embankment overflow proposed in this study are feasible. The assessment results provide a reference for effectively preventing and reducing the losses caused by wave storm surge embankment overflow disasters.
